title: Affirming the role of the United States in improving access to quality, inclusive public education and improving learning outcomes for children and adolescents, particularly for girls, around the world.
plain_summary: This resolution says the United States should help improve education for children around the world, especially girls and poor or disadvantaged kids. It recognizes that good education helps countries grow stronger and safer. The resolution asks the US government to keep being a leader in helping other countries build better schools and education programs. It wants the State Department and other US agencies to use all their tools to help more children get quality education worldwide.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution affirms the role of the United States in improving access to quality, inclusive public education and improving learning outcomes for children and adolescents, particularly for girls, around the world.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: January twenty-fourth has been declared as the International Day of Education to celebrate how education helps with development, economic growth, and global security. Access to good education reduces poverty, helps economies grow, creates peace and security, and improves public health. People earn nine percent more money for each extra year of school they complete. Every one dollar invested in girls' education can return up to two dollars and eighty cents, which equals billions of dollars in additional economic growth. When twice as many young people have secondary education (high school level), the risk of conflict is cut in half. Secondary education can reduce the chance that young people will support political violence by forty-eight percent. Currently, two hundred fifty-one million children around the world are not in school. Three out of four children in low-income and middle-income countries cannot read and understand a simple text. About four hundred seventy-three million children, more than one in six worldwide, live in areas affected by conflict. In two thousand twenty-four, forty-nine percent of all school-aged refugee children were out of school. Education saves lives during emergencies and can provide stability, access to important physical and mental health programs, and a chance to regain and grow important thinking skills. It also teaches essential survival skills like how to stay safe from unexploded bombs or how to prevent disease spread. Education during emergencies reduces the likelihood of child labor, trafficking, recruitment of children by military forces or armed groups, child marriage, and teenage pregnancy. Girls are two and a half times more likely to be out of school during emergency situations and are less likely to return to school after their education is disrupted. Disability is one of the most serious barriers to education, and there are two hundred forty million children with disabilities worldwide. In fiscal year two thousand twenty-three, the United States Agency for International Development's global education programs reached more than forty-four million learners through various educational programs and provided nutritious meals to three and a half million children across thirty-four countries, job training to one and a half million individuals, and educational help to more than nineteen and four tenths million young women and girls. United States support for partnerships, including the Global Partnership for Education and Education Cannot Wait, adds to United States Government programs and brings in additional resources for this important work. The United States Government has long been a leader in global education, and experience shows that when the United States Government steps back from critical development and humanitarian programs, it leaves gaps that other influences will fill. Congress passed the bipartisan Reinforcing Education Accountability in Development Act in two thousand seventeen and renewed that law in two thousand twenty-four for an additional five fiscal years to show the United States Government's commitment to improve access to education around the world and ensure people worldwide have the education and skills needed to be productive members of society and the workforce.

sections.title: What the House of Representatives Decides
sections.text: The House of Representatives commends the leadership and commitment of the United States Government to improve access to quality, inclusive education and improved learning outcomes for the poorest and most marginalized children and adolescents worldwide, which promotes global stability, economic prosperity, and poverty elimination. The House recognizes that the United States Government's basic education programs are strengthened and complemented by Global Partnership for Education's education systems-level approach and partnership building and Education Cannot Wait's focus on education in emergencies and extended crises. The House acknowledges that education is a lifesaving humanitarian intervention and encourages the United States Government to include education in all humanitarian response efforts and continue to promote coordination across all sectors and branches of the United States Government. The House urges the United States Government to meet its various commitments to international basic education through its annual budget requests to Congress, recognizing the significant global need. The House calls on the Secretary of State and the Administrator of the United States Agency for International Development to use all diplomatic, humanitarian, and development means to promote access to quality education for children and adolescents throughout the world. The House calls on the Secretary of State and the Administrator of the United States Agency for International Development to commit to prioritizing quality, inclusive public education for children and youth with disabilities, girls, children and youth in emergency and crisis settings, and traditionally marginalized groups as a critical component to learning equity, poverty elimination, and global stability and prosperity.
